chapters.title: A long time ago
chapters.text:
In 2024, for the first time in a US election, artificial intelligence may shape voters is choices as much as celebrity endorsements do. That kind of change has a big effect on information and social networks.

Knowing the same story can be enough to build trust between two strangers. As a society, we are hooked on stories. Today, that shows up most in what people post on Instagram, where stories spark emotion and pull people into something bigger than themselves. Maybe you heard that the Bieber family had a baby. If not, you must have worked hard to avoid the news. The whole internet was buzzing, and fans were thrilled, as if they had given birth themselves.

How many of those fans have met the Biebers in person. Very few. So why do they feel so close to the moment. Because the stories about the couple, including the drama, the wedding, and the pregnancy, touch their fans too. They do not need to have known Justin or Hailey from childhood to feel part of their lives.

Oddly, this points back to Christianity. The story of Jesus has tied people together for thousands of years, especially at Christmas and Easter, and it has let them share joy and sorrow together. What other good comes from knowing particular stories. Let is explore that together.

chapters.title: How to build a network
chapters.text:
Along with ideas like history, religion, or democracy, information is intersubjective. It is not fully objective, like nature, and not fully subjective, like pain, because it only exists when someone knows or remembers it.

This ability to believe in something together and set rules is the foundation for building information networks. Modern studies show that repeating a fake memory over and over eventually makes a person accept it as a real memory, as Yuval Noah Harari has noted.

Imagine you want to build a network that will bring many people together for the common good. What do you need to know to avoid past mistakes. You need a worthy and selfless reason that will pull people in. Stalinism and Nazism fail this test, because their goal was to raise obedient citizens with no opinions of their own. You also need your own myth or origin story that will inspire people and remind them of the core purpose. The story is fiction and can be updated. Without that, the group can drift into a cult or a dictatorship that shuts out new ideas. And you need rules that help you reach the goal and that do not pull the group away from its values. The Bible has passed a thousand year test for effectiveness, but your rules do not have to be one size fits all. The point is that they make sense for your community.

Everything can be information, but not all information is useful. In 2021, the astrology niche was worth almost thirteen billion dollars. For centuries, the stars have helped match Virgos with Aquarians, guided travelers, and even helped settle political disputes. Newspaper horoscopes and online forecasts do carry information, but whether any of it is useful is still up for debate.

chapters.title: Writing gave us more truth
chapters.text:
Many of us know someone who tells the same stories on repeat. Have you noticed that the details shift a little each time, with new bits, new phrases, and even new characters.

This is not just a modern habit. Across eras, people have changed the stories they tell, either because the original faded or because they wanted to dress it up. Pinning down those changes is hard.

For example, a mother in a Stone Age tribe tells her children, with sounds, that their father died in a tiger attack. How could anyone check. They could not. They would believe the tiger story, even if it had actually been a bear, since to her the roars were close enough.

That is why the invention of writing expanded the reach, tracking, storage, and spread of information. Bureaucracy and filing systems came next, since people realized written records had to be sorted and stored. Shared terms, folders, and libraries made finding the right data easier, but they also flattened out unique stories that were hard to classify.

Written promises or duties feel more serious. We can feel these challenges even today. Interesting things happened with the Talmud. Written knowledge made it easier for Jewish followers to keep the commandments in one place. New questions came up, like how to apply the rules in modern times. One example is the Sabbath rule against all work. Does using a phone or elevator count as work.

People realized they needed teachers and authorities to help them handle information, even after it had been sorted. It became easier to lean on rabbis, sages, scientists, and other experts than to argue about what a text meant.

A note worth flagging. Expertise, real or fake, has also caused plenty of trouble in human history. You will see how soon enough.

chapters.title: The pull of the absurd
chapters.text:
After writing came the European print revolution, which by 1600 let almost anyone publish anything in large amounts. This should have lifted public knowledge, since information was being copied much faster. But not all information is accurate. With the printing press, the world also spread conspiracy theories and fiction on a much bigger scale. You may know one of them, witchcraft.

In many communities, there were always people who knew more about nature, healing, and helping others. They were called shamans, healers, or witches, and only in the Middle Ages did such people start to look dangerous. The mystery around witchcraft grew more threatening and built an intersubjective reality. The public did not need proof that witches existed, kept company with the devil, seduced the innocent, and arrived naked to their meetings. Stories from tortured confessions in nearby villages did the job. Common sense was buried under layers of fake news and gossip.

Ignorance feeds blind trust in institutions that can start to misuse their power. The 1487 book Hammer of the Witches, written by the German inquisitor Heinrich Kramer, gained groundless weight. The methods it listed for spotting witches, torturing them, and punishing them became a manual for centuries. Witch hunters could have saved thousands of lives if they had asked why a follower of the Holy Word was so cruel toward a magical claim with no solid proof.

Once this new reality took hold, its followers, witch hunters, judges, and priests, gained jobs and fortunes, and left written records of their expeditions, trials, and ceremonies to drive Satan out of the accused. By the 17th century, when common sense returned and witch hunting finally faded, scientists were left with a huge pile of baseless documented "crimes" and no clear way to handle them.

chapters.title: The democratic pill
chapters.text:
When people say they resolved a conflict with little argument, they often mean a democratic fix. Let is look at how democratic and dictatorial societies have shaped access to information.

Becoming a dictator in the first tribes was nearly impossible. Leaders could try to claim a territory and limit hunting in it, but unhappy members could simply pack up and go berry picking and hunting elsewhere. Tribal leaders also lived in the same conditions and stayed close to their people. Anyone unhappy with their leadership could walk up and talk to them, unlike today, when a meeting might need an official letter or a security check.

A democratic society protects the rights of minorities as well as the majority. Over time, as states rose and fell, the marks of democracies and dictatorships became clearer. They differ in three main ways.

The first is self correcting mechanisms. Democratic systems can admit mistakes and offer changes. Dictators never make mistakes in their own eyes, and in a crisis they blame certain people or agencies for misreading orders.

The second is varied sources of information and authorities. A leader or party in power cannot be expert in every area of public life, so they have to listen to media, schools, and courts. In a dictatorship, any other platform with a different view risks exposing the regime is crimes and breaking the people is fear of the leaders. That was true in the USSR and still is in modern Russia.

The third is room for different groups of citizens. People hold different values and cannot all think alike. As long as they do not endanger others, they should have ways to live by their own plans, even if those plans clash with the leadership. Dictatorships brand anyone who disagrees as an enemy of the people.

Looking at how information has been used for good and bad in these settings helps us understand our own societies, both past and present.

chapters.title: Do you accept cookies
chapters.text:
How would you feel knowing someone was watching your every move, twenty four hours a day. What about someone knowing what you feel, think, and dream about, even the parts you cannot share with a partner or a friend.

Now take a close look at your screen. Over the last few years, laptops and phones have become full members of the family. Like every invention, they come with pros and cons.

Any physical action a person takes leaves a data trace, as Yuval Noah Harari has pointed out. Algorithms track what sites people sign up for, what tickets they buy, who they back in Facebook comments, and what they order for the holidays. We also hand over a lot by posting on social media. How often do you like a TikTok video or a reel. How often do you share a post and ask, how do they know this about us. The truth is, we show our cards to the world.

In modern democracies, careful surveillance, paired with privacy and human rights, can strengthen the system is self correcting features and improve security, like predicting crimes and tracking public health. Dictatorships use the same data to tighten their grip, control people, and sometimes build blackmail files on citizens.

The digital age wants to keep users online all the time. That has fed the rise of FOMO, the fear of missing out. Now a word on technology, and the main "enemy" of humanity in post apocalyptic movies, artificial intelligence.

In China in 2023, a facial recognition algorithm helped identify Yuechuan Lei, who had been kidnapped at age three in 2001. Thanks to well tuned code, parents who had searched for over twenty years were finally reunited with their son. A year earlier, Iranian authorities had started using the same kind of algorithm to identify and punish women who wore the hijab the wrong way or appeared in public without one.

We do not have to slip into technological determinism and complain that we are losing control of our devices. We do need to take responsibility for the purpose of the technology we build and for who benefits from it.

chapters.title: In closing
chapters.text:
Today, having information is not a guarantee of success or control over a situation. Even knowing the truth does not open every door. It only lets us see some parts of reality.

So let is pay closer attention to which information networks we belong to, which regimes we support, and what traces we leave online. A few starting steps can help. Ask your friends where they get their news and information, and check data from different sources to find the closest thing to the truth.

Think about which stories from books, movies, and other people is lives hit you the most. What draws you to them. Could any of them hide a manipulative spin on the facts. And look at the parts of your life that touch artificial intelligence. Learn how those systems work, so you can put them to work for you.
